_meta.editorial_scope: Draft event-selection review using only the active dossier. Candidate decisions are agent proposals for human review, not final seed selections.
_meta.selection_logic: Prioritize candidates that directly explain how local Bronx party culture became a wider hip-hop cultural form through place, time, sound/culture, and circulation. Treat broad context, high-attribution claims, external geography, and media-afterlife items cautiously unless they clearly serve the route thesis.
global_review_needs: Compare retrospective oral histories before using origin, first, invented, or causation language.
Verify place specificity before transferring any candidate into map-first seed data.
Keep commercial records, downtown attention, film, and broadcast circulation framed as transitions or representations, not cultural origins.
Review all media and image leads for rights, stability, attribution, and source quality before use.

review_clusters.title: Pre-1973 Context and Sedgwick Origin Memory
review_clusters.rationale: The Sedgwick candidate is the strongest place-specific route anchor, while the two earlier candidates explain conditions and prevent a single-origin reading. They should remain separate in review so context does not become an implied causal proof.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Use the context candidates to shape route framing or adjacent route notes unless specific, mappable, source-backed examples emerge.

review_clusters.title: Party Infrastructure, DJ Practice, and Community Spaces
review_clusters.rationale: These candidates together explain repeated practice across equipment, records, parks, schools, community centers, and clubs. They overlap but serve different route functions if source work can support specific places and chronology.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Prioritize candidates with verifiable locations, dates, flyers, oral histories, or archive records. Cedar Park and clubs need stronger specificity before seed transfer.

review_clusters.title: DJ Technique and Attribution Claims
review_clusters.rationale: General breakbeat and mobile sound practice can carry the route's technical argument without forcing unresolved first-invention claims. Grandmaster Flash and scratching attribution may remain separate only if source comparison supports careful wording.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Avoid first/invented claims unless multiple sources agree and exact wording is reviewed. Separate object/equipment claims from retrospective technique narratives.

review_clusters.title: MCing, Dance, Graffiti, and Party-Floor Culture
review_clusters.rationale: These candidates broaden the route beyond DJs and records. They should be developed only where the route can show relationships among sound, place, crews, audience participation, and media circulation.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Require representative examples, dates, and source leads before treating dance or graffiti as route events rather than contextual strands.

review_clusters.title: Community Organization and Urban Crisis Context
review_clusters.rationale: Both candidates can deepen the route's account of place, community, and memory, but both carry sensitivity or causality risks. The blackout especially should not become a causal shortcut.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Use cautious wording and compare urban history with hip-hop sources. Handle later institutional history and allegations sensitively if Bronx River organization material is developed.

review_clusters.title: Records, Labels, and Radio Circulation
review_clusters.rationale: These candidates mark the transition from live local culture to recorded and broadcast visibility. They should not displace the Bronx party-culture spine.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Keep the route argument focused on mediation, circulation, and public visibility. Avoid a discographic sequence unless each record or outlet clarifies the route thesis.

review_clusters.title: Downtown Exchange, Film, Television, and Mainstream Visibility
review_clusters.rationale: This cluster helps explain circulation and memory after local practice becomes visible through art spaces, film, television, and wider media. Several candidates risk pulling the MVP away from Bronx-centered map logic.
review_clusters.review_guidance: Develop only the strongest New York-centered media anchors first. Treat later mainstream film and global spread as possible route conclusion or later expansion.

candidates.working_title: Caribbean sound system context in New York
candidates.route_function: Provides pre-1973 context for migration, sound systems, records, amplification, and party practice that may have shaped Bronx DJ culture.
candidates.decision_rationale: Important route context, but the dossier flags that it may work better as framing than as a discrete map event. It should be kept in development only if source work can avoid one-way origin-transfer claims.
candidates.review_question: Can this be tied to specific New York communities, practices, or places without overstating direct causality?
risk_notes: Context may be better as route framing than a map event.
Avoid simple origin transfer.
Influence language requires careful source comparison.
candidates.next_action: Develop as route-context candidate; look for source-backed place specificity before considering event transfer.

candidates.working_title: Bronx record and party culture before the origin story
candidates.route_function: Prevents the route from beginning abruptly at one famous party by foregrounding local youth parties, records, equipment, and neighborhood social life.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong thesis support but currently broad. It should remain under review until source work identifies representative examples rather than generalized background.
candidates.review_question: Which source-backed parties, venues, organizers, records, or community spaces can make this candidate concrete?
risk_notes: Hard to source without overgeneralization.
Could duplicate Sedgwick context if not given a distinct role.
Needs care to avoid flattening Bronx communities.
candidates.next_action: Search dossier-approved source leads for specific examples, dates, and places; otherwise use as route framing.

candidates.working_title: Kool Herc, Cindy Campbell, and the Sedgwick party
candidates.route_function: Acts as a major place-specific memory anchor for the route while allowing the story to include organizing labor, sound practice, place, and contested origin language.
candidates.decision_rationale: This is the strongest candidate for a seed-development anchor because it has a specific place, year, named participants, public-memory significance, and a clear role in the route thesis. It must be framed as associated with an origin story, not as the sole birth of hip-hop.
candidates.review_question: What exact wording can acknowledge Sedgwick's public-memory role while avoiding sole-birthplace and one-person-origin framing?
risk_notes: Avoid sole-birthplace wording.
Verify Cindy Campbell's role and wording.
Retrospective and anniversary sources need comparison.
Do not make one person the entire origin story.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; build a source-comparison note for birthplace, organizer, DJ, date, and memory claims.

candidates.working_title: Mobile sound and breakbeat practice develop
candidates.route_function: Explains how equipment, records, dance response, amplification, and DJ repetition made local party practice into a recognizable cultural form.
candidates.decision_rationale: Central to the route's sound/culture thesis and useful for connecting place, technology, and audience participation. It is strong if written as a practice-development candidate rather than a single invention claim.
candidates.review_question: Which claims about breakbeat practice, equipment, and repetition are source-backed, and which should remain cautious?
risk_notes: Needs precise wording to avoid unsupported technique claims.
Technical claims can become mythic.
Avoid implying one party or one DJ created the practice alone.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; compare DJ interviews and equipment sources before drafting event language.

candidates.working_title: Schools, parks, and community centers host party culture
candidates.route_function: Shows hip-hop as repeated neighborhood practice across community spaces rather than a single origin event or club scene.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong route-spine candidate because it connects map-first exploration, community participation, and repeated practice. It needs specific sites or a justified regional representation before seed transfer.
candidates.review_question: Can the route identify specific schools, parks, community centers, dates, flyers, or oral histories to support this as an event or event cluster?
risk_notes: May require multiple places or a justified regional strategy.
Hard to map without specific sites and dates.
Must avoid treating communities as background for famous figures.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; gather candidate place examples and decide whether this becomes one regional event, multiple events, or route context.

candidates.working_title: Cedar Park and outdoor party culture
candidates.route_function: Tests a specific public-space anchor for outdoor parties, block-party culture, and neighborhood gathering.
candidates.decision_rationale: Promising because it may give the route a concrete public-space map point, but the dossier marks exact dates and claims as unresolved.
candidates.review_question: Which Cedar Park events, participants, dates, and sources can be verified?
risk_notes: Exact dates and claims need verification.
May overlap with the broader schools, parks, and community centers candidate.
Public-space claims need place-specific evidence.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; develop only if source work produces specific, attributable Cedar Park claims.

candidates.working_title: Grandmaster Flash and turntable technique
candidates.route_function: Makes DJ technique, equipment, and performance method visible as material culture within the route.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong candidate because it connects sound history to observable technique and source leads include institutional material. It should remain focused on technique and equipment, not generalized group history.
candidates.review_question: Which technique claims can be attributed to which source, and how should retrospective accounts be cross-checked?
risk_notes: Attribute techniques with source-specific wording.
Separate technique claims, group history, and later retrospective accounts.
Avoid unsupported first/invention language.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; build claim-level notes from Smithsonian, NAMM, and interview sources.

candidates.working_title: Scratching and DJ technique attribution
candidates.route_function: Allows the route to discuss technique, memory, contested invention claims, and multiple DJ contributions.
candidates.decision_rationale: Editorially useful but high-risk. It may be better as a comparison note attached to a broader DJ technique event unless source review supports a separate, carefully worded candidate.
candidates.review_question: Can this candidate be framed without making unresolved invented/first claims?
risk_notes: High risk around first/invented language.
Technique attribution requires source comparison.
Retrospective accounts may conflict.
candidates.next_action: Hold as maybe; compare attribution claims before deciding whether to keep separate or fold into a DJ technique narrative.

candidates.working_title: MCs, crews, and party hosting practices
candidates.route_function: Keeps MCing connected to live parties, hosting, call-and-response, crews, and audience interaction before commercial recordings dominate the story.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong route function because it balances DJ-centered framing and supports the transition from local performance to records. It needs named examples before seed transfer.
candidates.review_question: Which MCs, crews, recordings, flyers, or oral histories can represent this practice without overgeneralizing?
risk_notes: Candidate may need named examples before seed transfer.
Avoid treating MCing as only a later record-industry development.
Community and crew differences need careful wording.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; identify representative examples and source-backed chronology.

candidates.working_title: Dance crews and party-floor culture
candidates.route_function: Makes bodily practice, dance response, audience participation, and party space visible in the route.
candidates.decision_rationale: Important for a fuller cultural account, but the dossier flags a need for representative examples and dates. It should not be included merely as illustration.
candidates.review_question: Which dance crews, places, dates, films, or oral histories can support a route-specific event?
risk_notes: Need representative examples and dates.
Avoid treating dance as secondary illustration.
Rights risks likely if using film stills or performance images.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; seek specific examples and decide whether this is an event, context note, or media-linked strand.

candidates.working_title: Graffiti, trains, and visual culture
candidates.route_function: Explores hip-hop as visual and spatial culture moving through city surfaces, trains, crews, and later media or gallery circulation.
candidates.decision_rationale: Conceptually strong but map-model and route-fit questions remain unresolved. It needs a clear relationship to sound, place, community, or media to belong in this music-history route.
candidates.review_question: Can the route represent graffiti's mobile geography without forcing it into a single point event?
risk_notes: May need a nonstandard map strategy.
Avoid reducing graffiti to decoration for a music route.
Attribution, rights, identity, and context risks for images.
candidates.next_action: Hold as maybe; define map strategy and route relationship before seed transfer.

candidates.working_title: Bronx River crews and community organization
candidates.route_function: Researches crews, organization, neighborhood identity, and community space as part of scene formation.
candidates.decision_rationale: Potentially important for community organization and Bronx geography, but sensitive review is required and the dossier does not yet establish enough source specificity.
candidates.review_question: What source-backed claims can be made about Bronx River Houses, crews, organization, and community identity within the 1970s route frame?
risk_notes: Sensitive review needed for later institutional history.
Avoid flattening organization, resident context, and neighborhood identity.
Later allegations and institutional history require careful handling if relevant.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; source and sensitivity review required before any event-level development.

candidates.working_title: The 1977 blackout as context and myth
candidates.route_function: Allows the route to examine urban crisis, retrospective equipment narratives, and memory without treating crisis as a mechanical cause of hip-hop.
candidates.decision_rationale: Useful as source-criticism and urban-context material, but high risk as a standalone event because causality is contested and easily overstated.
candidates.review_question: Should this remain a context sidebar or memory note rather than an event candidate?
risk_notes: Do not use as a direct cause unless sources support the claim.
High risk of overstating causation.
Urban history sources are not automatically evidence for hip-hop claims.
candidates.next_action: Use as context unless source comparison justifies a carefully framed event about memory and myth.

candidates.working_title: Bronx clubs and local performance circuits
candidates.route_function: Tests whether indoor venues and local performance networks should sit alongside parks, schools, community centers, and outdoor parties.
candidates.decision_rationale: Useful for route continuity and performance geography, but it currently lacks named venues, addresses, and active-year verification.
candidates.review_question: Which Bronx clubs or venues can be verified with dates, addresses, performers, and route relevance?
risk_notes: Venue names, addresses, and active years need review.
May overlap with broader party-space candidate.
Youth access and local business claims need sourcing.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; develop named venue list before event-level review.

candidates.place: New York / New Jersey industry node
candidates.working_title: "Rapper's Delight" and commercial visibility
candidates.route_function: Marks a media and industry transition in which rap became more publicly visible through records, labels, mediation, and markets.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong endpoint or transition candidate because it helps show how live party practices entered commercial recording circuits. It must be clearly framed as visibility, not origin.
candidates.review_question: How should the route map the New York/New Jersey industry geography while staying centered on Bronx and New York route logic?
risk_notes: Commercial visibility is not origin.
New Jersey industry locations may require external-node strategy.
Record-industry framing can overshadow local practice.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; define map strategy and source-backed wording around commercial visibility.

candidates.working_title: Early rap records and independent labels
candidates.route_function: Broadens the record-industry transition beyond one record into distribution, independent labels, and public circulation.
candidates.decision_rationale: Useful context but risks becoming too discographic for the route. It should be developed only if it clarifies how local practice moved through media and markets.
candidates.review_question: Which records or labels are necessary to explain the route thesis rather than simply extending chronology?
risk_notes: Could become too discographic without a clear route argument.
Verify dates, personnel, chart claims, and relationship to local practice.
External geography creep is possible.
candidates.next_action: Hold as maybe; identify a small number of route-critical industry examples or use as context.

candidates.working_title: Radio DJs and broadcast circulation
candidates.route_function: Explores how local sound and records reached wider audiences through broadcast channels and public listening.
candidates.decision_rationale: Potentially valuable for circulation, but broadcast reach may not map cleanly and source specificity is unresolved.
candidates.review_question: Which stations, DJs, broadcast moments, or archives can support a mappable and source-aware candidate?
risk_notes: Broadcast reach may not map cleanly.
Rights, dates, station metadata, and weak-match risk for media leads.
Media framing may distort community priorities.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; verify station/site strategy and source quality before event development.

candidates.working_title: "The Message" and social commentary
candidates.route_function: Tests a connection between recorded rap, urban narrative, and social conditions within the broader route timeframe.
candidates.decision_rationale: Important record-history candidate, but the dossier explicitly questions whether it belongs in this route or a later records-focused pass.
candidates.review_question: Does this candidate clarify the Birth of Hip-Hop route thesis, or does it belong in a separate route about recorded rap and social commentary?
risk_notes: Decide whether this belongs in this route or a later records-focused pass.
Urban-condition framing needs careful causality.
Record focus may pull attention from local party culture.
candidates.next_action: Hold as maybe; review against route scope before source expansion.

candidates.working_title: Downtown clubs, art spaces, and hip-hop exchange
candidates.route_function: Researches cross-scene circulation among clubs, galleries, performers, writers, media workers, and new audiences.
candidates.decision_rationale: Useful for circulation and media transition, but currently needs named places and stronger guardrails against implying downtown validation.
candidates.review_question: Which specific downtown venues, participants, and exchanges can be sourced without framing downtown attention as legitimizing hip-hop?
risk_notes: Needs named places and avoids validation framing.
Avoid implying downtown attention made hip-hop legitimate.
Community-source comparison needed.
candidates.next_action: Keep as maybe; develop only with specific venues, dates, and source-backed exchange claims.

candidates.working_title: *Wild Style* and film documentation
candidates.route_function: Explores film as documentation, staging, performance, and circulation of overlapping hip-hop practices.
candidates.decision_rationale: Strong media-circulation candidate because it can connect performance, graffiti, dance, film, and route memory. It must distinguish documentation from fictionalized or staged material.
candidates.review_question: Which production, setting, performance, release, and reception claims are source-backed and relevant to the route?
risk_notes: Separate documentary value from fictionalized or staged scenes.
Film rights and clip availability require review.
Needs source-backed venue and participant links if tied to downtown exchange.
candidates.next_action: Keep for development; separate film metadata, represented practices, and circulation claims.

candidates.working_title: *Style Wars* and television documentation
candidates.route_function: Opens research into graffiti, dance, broadcast television, public interpretation, and visual culture within hip-hop's wider visibility.
candidates.decision_rationale: Potentially useful as a contrast with film documentation, but its fit with the route's music-history center needs review.
candidates.review_question: Does this candidate advance the music-history route, or should it remain visual-culture context?
risk_notes: Verify fit with the route's music-history focus.
Must distinguish production methods and cultural focus.
Rights and route-fit review required.
candidates.next_action: Hold as maybe; review alongside graffiti and film candidates before deciding event status.

candidates.working_title: Mainstream film and hip-hop visibility
candidates.route_function: Would test whether commercial film representations help explain hip-hop's wider spread after early documentation.
candidates.decision_rationale: Reject for current route development because the dossier itself flags this as possible later expansion, and it risks pulling the MVP away from Bronx-centered route logic. The material may remain useful in future media-circulation work.
candidates.review_question: Should this be revisited in a later route about mainstream media representation rather than the first Birth of Hip-Hop route?
risk_notes: May be later expansion if it pulls away from Bronx route logic.
Commercial media can overshadow local practice.
Film image and clip rights would need review.
candidates.next_action: Do not develop for current event list; preserve as future-route or later-expansion lead.

candidates.working_title: Tours, records, and media spread hip-hop beyond New York
candidates.route_function: Would represent outward circulation beyond New York and point toward the route's broader endpoint.
candidates.decision_rationale: Reject as an event candidate for the current MVP route because the geography and scope exceed the New York-centered, Bronx-first map logic. It may work as a concluding route note if later supported.
candidates.review_question: Can outward spread be summarized as route conclusion without adding a weak global map event?
risk_notes: May be a route conclusion rather than a map event.
Could exceed the New York map focus.
External geography creep and rights/source-quality risks remain unresolved.
candidates.next_action: Do not develop as current event candidate; consider only as cautious route-end context.
